background: Exercise 11.11 calculated the mean length of menstrual cycles in an SRS of $n=9$ women. The data revealed $\bar{x}=27.78$ days with standard deviation $s=2.906$ days.

task.query: Calculate a $95 \%$ confidence interval for the mean menstrual cycle length.
task.answer: The confidence interval can be calculated using the formula $\bar{x} \pm t^* \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$, where $t^*$ is the critical value from the t-distribution for $df=n-1$ at $95 \%$ confidence level.

task.query: Based on the confidence interval you just calculated, is the mean menstrual cycle length significantly different from 28.5 days at $\alpha=0.05$ (two sided)? Is it significantly different from $\mu=30$ days at the same $\alpha$-level? Explain your reasoning.
task.answer: To test significance, check if 28.5 or 30 falls within the calculated confidence interval. If either value falls outside the interval, it is significantly different at $\alpha=0.05$ level.

data: Exercise 11.11 ：Menstrual cycle lengths (days) in an SRS of nine women are as follows: $\{31,28,26,24,29,33,25,26,28\}$. Use this data to test whether mean menstrual cycle length differs significantly from a lunar month. (A lunar month is 29.5 days.) Assume population values vary according to a Normal distribution. Use a twosided alternative. Show all hypothesis-testing steps.
